reasoning: The vulnerable code path (filter_counts_query at line 153) is gated only by isProductSearchPage(), which checks three public GET parameters (?dgwt_wcas=1&post_type=product&s=...) — no nonce, no capability check, no authentication required. Any unauthenticated visitor can satisfy that page-level condition. However, the actual SQL injection requires a second plugin (or theme/mu-plugin) to hook 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids' and return non-integer elements. In the canonical plugin-only flow, getProductIds() returns $postsIDsBuffer populated from WP_Post->ID values obtained via get_posts(), which are PHP integers from a DB-backed WordPress API — these are safe. The only path to inject non-integer values is through the public WordPress filter 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids', which any co-installed plugin can hook. Installing a plugin in WordPress requires manage_options / install_plugins capability — an administrator-level privilege. An unauthenticated or subscriber-level attacker cannot install plugins and therefore cannot place the hook that injects malicious values. The gate is not a nonce or cryptographic secret — it is the requirement for a second admin-installed plugin that itself contains or exposes a vulnerability. This is a second-order / API design flaw: the filter lacks integer sanitization at the consumption point, so any future or current co-plugin regression would flow directly into raw SQL with no mitigation. Because exploitation requires admin-level plugin installation, gate_breachable=false and severity is downgraded to LOW.
